summary: This notebook demonstrates an inference-only pipeline for the BirdCLEF 2025 competition, combining predictions from two pre-trained audio classification models (ConvNeXtV2-Nano and ECA-NFNet-L0). It processes 5-second audio windows into mel spectrograms, runs inference with optional test-time augmentation, and applies targeted post-processing techniques like low-rank power adjustment and temporal smoothing. The author emphasizes that blending diverse architectures and tuning post-processing steps can significantly boost leaderboard scores, though weight adjustments are better suited for private notebooks to avoid leakage. Readers should learn how to structure a robust inference workflow and apply effective post-processing for audio classification tasks.
competition_flow: Loads pre-trained ConvNeXtV2 and NFNet models, processes test audio into mel spectrograms, generates per-chunk species predictions, applies power adjustment and temporal smoothing, and blends the outputs into a final submission CSV.
data_reading: Reads .ogg test audio files using librosa.load and torchaudio.load.
Extracts class labels from directory names in the training audio folder.
data_processing: Converts to mel spectrograms using librosa and torchaudio with varying parameters (n_fft=2048/1024, n_mels=512/128).
Resizes spectrograms to 256x256 for the ConvNeXtV2 path.
Applies test-time augmentations (horizontal and vertical flips on spectrograms).
Post-processes predictions with low-rank power adjustment on low-confidence columns and temporal smoothing across adjacent chunks.
ensembling: Blends predictions from two models using weights of 0.6 for NFNet and 0.4 for ConvNeXtV2, followed by low-rank power adjustment on low-confidence predictions and temporal smoothing across 5-second audio chunks.
insights: Using diverse model architectures improves robustness and leaderboard performance in audio classification tasks.
Post-processing techniques like power adjustment and temporal smoothing can effectively boost scores without retraining.
Adjusting blending weights should be done in private notebooks to prevent data leakage and maintain fairness.
